papers.title: Wheel–rail-induced derailment analysis: a comprehensive literature review of experimental and simulation-based approaches | Railway Engineering Science | Springer Nature Link
papers.abstract: This review highlights the history of research conducted on derailment studies due to the wheel and rail interface. Several parameters influence the wheel–rail contact patch, including wheel and rail profiles, wheel and rail wear and defects, third-body layers, contaminations, and most notably, the contact forces (lateral, vertical, and longitudinal) [74 Wheel–rail interface handbook. CRC Press, Boca Raton")]. The contact patch is also influenced by the general direction of lateral and vertical contact forces on the wheels traversing a tangent track, such as shown for curved and tangent track

papers.title: Simplified criteria to assess flange climbing derailment | Railway Engineering Science | Springer Nature Link
papers.abstract: Weinstock proposed a derailment criterion that considers the influence of both wheels on derailment, as opposed to focussing solely on the wheel susceptible to climbing. The logic of this derailment criterion is that when the FW begins to climb, the NFW, if connected by a rigid axle, will experience a downward sliding motion. This implies that the force required for one wheel to climb must overcome the resistive forces at both wheel–rail contact points. To derive this equation, both contact scenarios shown in Fig. 1 were analysed. The condition for the right wheel to climb is the same as Nadal

papers.title: On the investigation of wheel flange climb derailment mechanism and methods to control it | Molatefi | Journal of Theoretical and Applied Mechanics
papers.abstract:
Habibollah Molatefi, Amin Mazraeh

In this research, investigations are focused on the study of railway vehicle wheel flange climb derailment. A three dimensional nonlinear dynamic model of the wheel-set and suspension system is developed. Having validated the model through field tests, the effect of friction coefficient, wheel-set AOA, vehicle suspension system and running speed on the wheel flange climb derailment are investigated. In addition, different rail lubrication methods are studied and their effects on the wheel flange climb derailment are compared in the case of two point contact f

papers.abstract: For derailment forms such as JD and JCCD, the prerequisites for vehicle derailment are as follows: wheel lifting causes the lowest point of the wheel flange to exceed the top center of the rail, accompanied by significant lateral relative motion between the wheel and rail, resulting in the wheel descending into the dangerous contact zone. Therefore, in these cases, derailment behavior is jointly controlled by the vertical and lateral wheel–rail contact states. By considering the variation process of wheel–rail contact states in such derailment forms, the derailment risk of each contact state i

papers.abstract: is very soft the global track stiffness will be low (Li and Berggren, 2010). High global track stiffness generally corresponds to stiff ballast, sub-ballast, subgrade and rail pads. Conditions between these extremes are desired. Further, stiffness variations along the track should be avoided. Typical places along the track where variations in stiffness occur are in transition areas between bridges and embankment, at pile decks and at switches and turnouts (Dahlberg 2010). papers.abstract: Due to significant gauge face wear on the high rail, the wheel/rail contact patch was shifted further to field than is optimal, effectively lowering the L/V rollover threshold. The high lateral resilience of the low rail due to the recent tie and fastener renewal further concentrated gage-widening forces on the high rail. With the train already running underbalance, the additional lateral force from in-train buff forces concentrated further on the high rail. This unlikely, but not impossible, confluence of factors caused the high rail to roll outward (to field) under the second lead locomotive

papers.title: Wheelclimb Derailment Processes and Derailment Criteria.
papers.abstract: r I L_ -EXECUTIVE SUMMARY Introduction Statement of Problem: The most widely accepted criterion for wheelclimb derailment defines an upper limit on wheel/rail contact forces on the climbing wheel, the limit varying with the time duration of the forces. The reliability of this criterion in predicting derailment is of major importance, since it is used to evaluate the derailment safety of railroad vehicles, operating conditions, and track structures. This report presents an analytical and experimental evaluation of time-dependent wheel load derailment criteria for wheelclimb, which shows that th

papers.title: Assessment of safety against derailment using simulations and vehicle acceptance tests: a worldwide comparison of state-of-the-art assessment methods | Semantic Scholar
papers.abstract:
The assessment of derailment safety of new railway vehicle designs is a fundamental concern worldwide. Although the methods used for assessment in different countries and regions vary considerably, the fundamental mechanisms being addressed are the same. This paper provides a detailed review of the current state-of-the-art methods for derailment safety assessment in several regions and countries including Europe, UK, USA, and Russia. Brief overviews of the methods used in China, Korea, and…

papers.title: Wheel–rail-induced derailment analysis: a comprehensive literature review of experimental and simulation-based approaches
papers.abstract: This paper offers a comprehensive review of the literature on the role of wheel–rail contact mechanics in train derailments. Using experimental and simulation methods, this review examines various research efforts that analyze how contact forces influence wheel climb and derailment dynamics. The related studies are summarized, and insights are provided on how they have contributed to understanding derailments and enhancing overall rolling stock safety. The review shows significant progress across different specialized areas within the broader topic of derailments. This includes advanced, state

papers.title: Railway Safety Legal Challenges: Post-Colorado Derailment Analysis
papers.abstract:
Key Points: 

The fatal derailment of a BNSF Railway train in Colorado, resulting in the death of a truck driver and significant infrastructure damage, brings to light the critical role of regular and thorough railway inspections. Federal investigators are probing whether lapses in BNSF Railway’s inspection and maintenance practices contributed to the accident. 

This incident serves as a grim reminder of the catastrophic consequences of potential negligence in railway operations. Legal experts may scrutinize the adherence of BNSF Railway to safety protocols and industry standards. The tragedy

research_gaps: Limited ML/AI application to derailment probability prediction
Insufficient digital twin models for real-time track monitoring
Limited sensor-fusion approaches for early derailment warning
Sparse research on derailment risk in mixed-speed traffic corridors
Emerging need for autonomous inspection system validation frameworks
key_findings: Derailment safety in railway engineering is influenced by wheel-rail contact mechanics, including factors like wheel and rail profiles, wear, and contact forces
Recent research focuses on simulation and experimental approaches to improve derailment prediction
